data.history: Al Hashemi (الهاشمي) is an Arabic surname originally designating members or descendants of the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe, the same clan to which the Islamic prophet Muhammad belonged through his great-grandfather Hashim ibn Abd Manaf. The name appears across various regions with different transliterations including Al-Hashimi, Hashemi, Hashimi, or Hashmi in Persian contexts. The research provides extensive documentation of one prominent branch through Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani (1934-2017), whose family originated from the village of Bahraman near Rafsanjan in Kerman Province, Iran. His ancestors were Lurs from the Mir tribe of Bala Gariva who had migrated and settled in Bahraman, with his father Mirza Ali Hashemi Behramani being a wealthy pistachio merchant and one of Kerman's famous businessmen. The Rafsanjani branch took their name from Akbar Hashemi's great-grandfather, whose last name was Hashem, and became one of Iran's most influential political and business families through the 20th and 21st centuries.
data.description: Al Hashemi is an Arabic surname claiming descent from the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe, with documented branches including the prominent Iranian Rafsanjani family descended from Lur migrants to Kerman Province.
data.originStory: The surname Al-Hashemi originally designated members or descendants of the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe, named after Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, great-grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. The documented Iranian branch descended from Lurs of the Mir tribe of Bala Gariva who migrated and settled in Bahraman village near Rafsanjan in Kerman Province.
data.tribalOrigin: Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe (for the surname origin); Mir tribe of Bala Gariva (for the documented Iranian branch)
data.modernStatus: The documented Iranian branch became highly influential in politics and business, with Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani serving as Iran's fourth president (1989-1997) and chairman of the Expediency Discernment Council. The family owned Islamic Azad University (worth $20-25 billion) and maintained extensive business interests including properties in Dubai's Burj Khalifa.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Hashemi is the pronoun form of Hashim (Arabic: هاشم), named after Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, great-grandfather of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. The Rafsanjani branch took their name from Akbar Hashemi's great-grandfather, whose last name was Hashem.

data.history: The Al Obaidli family is referenced as one of the actual Arab Huwala tribes, specifically mentioned alongside the Qawasem, Hammadi, Al Nasur/Nassour, and Bani Tamim tribes that constitute the Arab El-Sahel (Arabs of the Eastern Coast). According to historical documentation, the Huwala were Arabian Iranians of tribal lineage who initially migrated to Iran in the 13th and 14th centuries, continuing their migrations until around the 19th century to areas that are now Iran's Hormozgan and Fars provinces, mainly around Bandar Abbas, Qishm, and the mainland near Bandar Lengeh. The Al Obaidli, as part of the original Arab Huwala tribes, followed Sunni Islam like most Peninsular Arabs, distinguishing them from the majority Persian Twelver Shia population in Iran. Most Huwala families, including the Al Obaidli, remigrated back to the Arabian Peninsula between the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the imposition of restrictive economic policies by Reza Shah in the 1930s leading to the migration of most remaining Huwala back to the Arabian Peninsula.
data.description: The Al Obaidli are identified as one of the original Arab Huwala tribes, part of the Arabian Iranians who migrated to southern Iran and later returned to the Arabian Peninsula. They are distinguished as actual Arab Huwalas rather than Achomi families.
data.originStory: The Al Obaidli are documented as one of the original Arab tribes that migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to southern Iran between the 13th-19th centuries as part of the Huwala tribal confederation, later returning to the Arabian Peninsula in the 19th-20th centuries.

data.history:
Based on the research provided, Al Bu Mundhir appears to be connected to the broader Banu Amir tribal confederation, a large and ancient Arab tribe that originated from Western Arabia and dominated Najd for centuries after the rise of Islam. The Banu Amir was an independent branch of the Hawazin confederation, with their original homeland being the border area between Najd and Hejaz in Khurmah and Ranyah. The name 'Al Bu Mundhir' likely derives from al-Mundhir ibn Amr, a notable figure mentioned in Islamic historical sources as a leader who was sent by Prophet Muhammad to call the people of Najd to Islam, earning the nickname 'Freed to die' according to historical accounts by Tabari.

The Banu Amir confederation included several major branches: Banu Kilab, Banu Numayr, Banu Ka'b (which included Banu Uqayl, Banu Ja'dah, Banu Qushayr, and Al Harish), Banu Hilal, Bani Yas, and Bani Amer. These tribes were native to al-Yamamah and Najd, with some sections later migrating to various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. The research indicates that sections of Banu Ka'b left al-Yamamah and Nejd at later dates and settled along both sides of the Persian Gulf, now found in the Ahwaz region of Iran, UAE, and Iraq.

While the research establishes the historical significance of the Banu Amir confederation and mentions al-Mundhir ibn Amr as a key historical figure, specific details about the Al Bu Mundhir family's migration patterns, settlement in the Gulf region, or their modern business and political activities are not provided in the available research materials.
data.description: Al Bu Mundhir appears to be connected to the ancient Banu Amir tribal confederation that originated from Western Arabia and dominated Najd after the rise of Islam. The name likely derives from the historical figure al-Mundhir ibn Amr, a leader mentioned in Islamic sources.
data.originStory: The name Al Bu Mundhir likely traces back to al-Mundhir ibn Amr, a leader from Banu Sa'ida who was sent by Prophet Muhammad to call the people of Najd to Islam. data.history: The Al Mannaie name traces its origins to medieval Italy, specifically to Guccio di Mannaia (also spelled Malnaia, Malnaggia, Manaie, Mannaie), an Italian goldsmith from Siena active from 1288 to 1322. He came from a family of Sienese goldsmiths and created the famous Chalice of Nicholas IV between 1288-1292, which contained the first documented use of translucent enamels using the basse-taille technique. The name variations (Mannaia, Manaie, Mannaie) appear in historical records from this period, with Guccio's signature appearing as 'Guccius Mannaie de Senis fecit' on his chalice. The surname Mannaie later evolved as a French family name, with records showing Richard Mennet in Normandy in 1180-1185 and various spelling variations including Manet, Mannet, Manez, Manai, Manaie, Mannai, and Mannaie. The modern Al Mannaie Group was established in 1981 and is headquartered in Dubai, becoming one of the largest conglomerates based in the United Arab Emirates, though the research provides no information connecting the medieval Italian/French origins to the modern Gulf business family.
data.description: A family name with documented medieval Italian origins through goldsmith Guccio di Mannaia (1288-1322), later appearing in French records, with a modern business conglomerate Al Mannaie Group established in Dubai in 1981.
data.originStory: The name originates from medieval Siena, Italy, through Guccio di Mannaia, a goldsmith from a family of Sienese goldsmiths active in the late 13th and early 14th centuries. The name later appeared in French genealogical records with various spellings.
data.modernStatus: Al Mannaie Group was established in 1981 and is headquartered in Dubai, operating as one of the largest conglomerates in the United Arab Emirates, with focus on retail and leisure experiences through subsidiaries like Dura Jumeirah Company.
data.nameEtymology: The name Mannaie is described as a nickname type surname derived from the Middle-High-German word 'man', from which the English word 'man' is derived, typically used for a fierce or strong man.

data.history:
The textile souk families represent the historical merchant communities that developed around Dubai's Dubai Textile Souk, also known as the Old Textile Souk, located in the historic Bur Dubai neighbourhood along Dubai Creek. The souk was constructed during the late 19th century under the progressive leadership of Maktoum bin Hashar in 1894, who encouraged commerce and trading. By 1902, Dubai experienced a significant influx of Iranian traders and Arab settlers, establishing what became the largest souks in Arabia at the time. The textile souk became a major commercial hub for textile exchange, serving both functional clothing needs and special occasions throughout the Persian Gulf region.

The families associated with the textile trade were predominantly of Indian and Pakistani origin, forming distinct communities within Bur Dubai. These merchant families settled in the region due to Dubai's long history of trade with India and Persia, taking advantage of Dubai Creek's strategic location for cargo ships from the Persian Gulf. By the mid-19th century, Bur Dubai housed approximately 100 Indian traders, while the broader Dubai area accommodated a diverse mix of Arabs, Persians, and Baluchis. The animated sikkas (narrow alleys) and shops in the souk still bear the names of ancient merchant families who immigrated from places such as Persia and India.

The textile souk families faced significant challenges during the worldwide recession of the 1930s post-World War I and the subsequent World War II period, when trade along Dubai Creek came to a halt. However, they recovered following the discovery of oil in the region, which sparked major economic growth and increased immigration. The growth in Indian and Pakistani populations gave new life to the Dubai Textile Souk, though its role gradually shifted from actual trade to tourism as Dubai modernized. Today, many descendants of these original merchant families continue to operate in the souk, though the focus has largely shifted to serving tourists while maintaining some traditional trading practices.
data.description: Historical merchant families of Indian, Pakistani, and Persian origin who established and operated the textile trade in Dubai's Old Textile Souk from the late 19th century, forming distinct communities in Bur Dubai.
data.originStory: The textile souk families originated from merchant communities in India, Pakistan, and Persia who migrated to Dubai during the 19th and early 20th centuries to engage in textile trade along the strategic Dubai Creek trading route.
data.modernStatus: Many families continue to operate in the Dubai Textile Souk, though the business has largely shifted from wholesale textile trading to tourist-oriented retail, with some maintaining traditional practices while adapting to modern tourism demands.
data.nameEtymology: The families are named after their association with the Dubai Textile Souk, which derives from the Arabic 'سوق الغزل والنسيج' (souk al-ghazl wal-nasij), meaning textile and weaving market.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Indian and Persian traders to establish textile trade operations along Dubai Creek

data.history: The Al Naqi family traces its origins to the 9th century Islamic religious figure Ali al-Hadi (c. 828-868), known by the title al-Naqi (meaning 'the distinguished'), who was the tenth Imam in Twelver Shia Islam. Born in Sorayya, a village near Medina founded by his great-grandfather Musa al-Kazim, Ali al-Hadi was summoned around 848 from Medina to the Abbasid capital of Samarra by Caliph al-Mutawakkil, where he was held under close surveillance until his death in 868. His son Hasan al-Askari (c. 844-874) succeeded him as the eleventh Imam, also living under house arrest in Samarra. According to Twelver Shia belief, Hasan al-Askari had a son, Muhammad al-Mahdi, who entered occultation (hiding) after his father's death in 874 and is believed by Twelvers to be the awaited Mahdi who will return in the end times. The Al Naqi lineage thus represents one of the most significant religious dynasties in Shia Islam, with their legacy centered on the Al-Askari Shrine in Samarra, Iraq, which houses the tombs of both Ali al-Hadi and Hasan al-Askari and remains a major pilgrimage site for Shia Muslims worldwide.
data.description: The Al Naqi family descends from Ali al-Hadi (828-868), the tenth Imam in Twelver Shia Islam, known by the title al-Naqi meaning 'the distinguished.' Their religious significance extends through his son Hasan al-Askari to the believed twelfth Imam Muhammad al-Mahdi.
data.originStory: The family originates from Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi, born around 828 CE in Sorayya, a village near Medina founded by his great-grandfather Musa al-Kazim. Ali al-Hadi was the son of Muhammad al-Jawad, the ninth Shia Imam, and his mother Samana, a freed slave of Maghrebi origin. The family's religious authority was established through direct descent from the Islamic prophet Muhammad through the Shia Imamic line.
data.modernStatus: The Al Naqi legacy is primarily preserved through religious institutions and the Al-Askari Shrine in Samarra, Iraq, which serves as a major center for Shia pilgrimage. The shrine has faced modern challenges, including bombing attacks by extremist groups in 2006 and 2007.
data.nameEtymology: Al Naqi derives from the Arabic title 'al-Naqi' meaning 'the distinguished' or 'the pure,' which was given to Ali al-Hadi, the tenth Shia Imam, alongside his other title 'al-Hadi' meaning 'the guide.'

data.history: The Al Zain family name derives from "Zain" meaning "ornament" or "beauty" in Arabic, most famously associated with Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Sajjad (c. 658-712), known as Zayn al-Abidin ("ornament of worshippers"). Ali al-Sajjad was the great-grandson of Prophet Muhammad and the fourth Imam in Shia Islam, who survived the Battle of Karbala in 680 and led a scholarly, devotional life in Medina until his death around 712. The research shows connections between various Middle Eastern families claiming descent from early Islamic figures, including the Al-Sadr family of Lebanon which traces lineage through Musa al-Kazim to Ali ibn Abi Talib. However, the research does not provide specific information about a contemporary Gulf family called "Al Zain" - their geographic origins, migration patterns, or modern activities. While the name has clear Islamic historical significance through the fourth Shia Imam, any direct genealogical connection to modern Gulf families of this name cannot be established from the available sources. The research focuses primarily on the historical figure Ali al-Sajjad and other unrelated families with different surnames.
data.description: Al Zain is an Arabic name meaning "ornament" historically associated with the fourth Shia Imam Ali al-Sajjad (Zayn al-Abidin). No specific information about contemporary Gulf families bearing this name is available in the research.

data.history: The Al Mahmoudi family traces its origins to the Qadhadhfa tribe, described as a small, relatively uninfluential Bedouin Arab tribe from the deserts of Tripolitania in western Libya. The family's most prominent member was Muammar Gaddafi, born around 1942 near Sirte to Mohammad Abdul Salam bin Hamed bin Mohammad (known as Abu Meniar) and Aisha bint Niran. His father was a poor goat and camel herder who made great sacrifices to provide his son with education despite their impoverished circumstances. The family followed traditional nomadic Bedouin lifestyle and were illiterate, keeping no birth records. Gaddafi's upbringing in this tribal culture profoundly influenced his later political ideology and personal preferences, as he continued to favor desert retreats throughout his life. Following Gaddafi's rise to power through the 1969 coup that established the Libyan Arab Republic, he increasingly relied on members of his Qadhadhfa tribe for security, particularly after discovering plots against him in the 1970s. The family's prominence was entirely tied to Gaddafi's political ascendancy and ended with his death during the 2011 Libyan Civil War.
data.description: The Al Mahmoudi family originates from the Qadhadhfa tribe, a small Bedouin Arab tribe from western Libya's desert regions. They gained prominence solely through Muammar Gaddafi, who ruled Libya from 1969 until his death in 2011.
data.originStory: The family comes from the Qadhadhfa, described as a small, relatively uninfluential Bedouin Arab tribe from the deserts of Tripolitania in Italian western Libya. They were traditional nomadic herders living in poverty, with Gaddafi's father working as a goat and camel herder.
data.modernStatus: The family's political prominence ended with Muammar Gaddafi's death in 2011 during the Libyan Civil War. Several of his sons were killed during the conflict, and the family lost all political power following the fall of his regime.

data.history: The Al Kharashi family has documented connections to Islamic scholarship spanning multiple continents and centuries. The most prominent historical connection is through Sheikh (K)ẖarāshi bn Muhammad Thāni (d. 1965), who served as a teacher to Sheikh Murtaḍa Gatta in Nigeria. This suggests the Al Kharashi name has been associated with Islamic education and religious leadership in West Africa during the 20th century. A more ancient connection exists with Muhammad al-Kharashi (1601-1690), an Egyptian Islamic scholar who became the first Grand Imam of Al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo. Al-Kharashi came from the village of Abu Kharash in Egypt's Buhaira Governorate, which is the etymological source of the family name. His influence extended across the Islamic world, reaching West Africa, where his scholarly works and methodology were transmitted through successive generations of Islamic teachers. The name also appears in modern institutional contexts, as evidenced by the Kharashi High School established in 1945 as the first modern Arabic and Islamic school in Yoruba land, Nigeria.
data.description: The Al Kharashi family name is historically associated with Islamic scholarship and education, with documented connections spanning from medieval Egypt to 20th century West Africa.
data.originStory: The name Al Kharashi derives from the village of Abu Kharash in Egypt's Buhaira Governorate, as confirmed by the 17th century scholar Muhammad al-Kharashi who was called Al-Kharashi because he came from this village.
data.modernStatus: The Al Kharashi name continues to be associated with Islamic education through institutions like Kharashi High School in Nigeria, established in 1945 as the first modern Arabic and Islamic school in Yoruba land.

data.history: The Al Balawi family name derives from the Balawi tribe, which has ancient Arabian origins tracing back to the Beersheba region in what is now the Negev desert. Historical records show the tribe has produced notable figures across different eras and regions. In medieval times, Abu Zama'a al-Balawi, known as Sidi Sahib and reportedly a companion of Prophet Muhammad, was buried in Kairouan, Tunisia in 654 AD after dying in battle, with his shrine becoming one of the city's most important religious sites by the 14th century. The family name appears across the Arab world in various contexts - from 12th-century Andalusian travelers like Abou Haggag Youssef Ibn Mohammed el-Balawi el-Andaloussi who documented the Lighthouse of Alexandria in 1166, to modern times with the controversial figure of Humam Khalil Abu-Mulal al-Balawi, a Jordanian doctor of Palestinian tribal origins who carried out the 2009 Camp Chapman attack against the CIA in Afghanistan. In the Gulf region, the Al Balawi name appears in Saudi Arabia, where Mansour Al-Balawi emerged as a prominent businessman and served as chairman of Al-Ittihad football club from 2003-2007, and Awwad Eid Al-Aradi Al-Balawi served as Director General of Saudi Arabian Border Guards.
data.description: Al Balawi is an Arabian family name with ancient tribal origins from the Beersheba region, with documented presence across the Arab world from medieval Andalusia and Tunisia to modern Jordan and Saudi Arabia.
data.originStory: According to historical records, the Al Balawi family traces its tribal origins to the Beersheba region in what is now the Negev desert of Israel. The tribe's ancestral roots are described as Palestinian, with the family originating from this specific geographic area of historical Palestine.
data.modernStatus: In Saudi Arabia, the Al Balawi family includes prominent businessmen like Mansour Al-Balawi, considered one of the richest people in the kingdom with diverse companies and real estate inherited from his father, and government officials like Awwad Eid Al-Aradi Al-Balawi who served as Director General of Saudi Arabian Border Guards.

data.history: The Gargash family is a prominent trading family in Dubai that is part of the minority Huwala community in the United Arab Emirates. The family has established itself as a significant business enterprise through Gargash Enterprise, which operated from 1995 to 2006. The family's patriarch, Mohamed Abdulkhaliq Gargash, died in 2016 and was honored with a mosque built in his name in the Al Quoz district of Dubai, completed in 2021. The family has produced notable figures in UAE politics and diplomacy, most prominently Anwar Mohammed Gargash, who served as minister of state for foreign affairs from February 2008 to February 2021 and subsequently became a senior diplomatic advisor to the UAE president. The Gargash name has become synonymous with both successful business ventures and high-level government service in the UAE.

data.history: The Al Rashedi family has documented political involvement in Qatar, specifically in the Al Ghuwariyah area of Al Khor Municipality. The most prominent member identified is Saeed Mubarak Al Rashedi, who won a seat in Qatar's Central Municipal Council elections in 2011, defeating the incumbent Nasser Abdullah Al Kaabi who had held the constituency no. 29 seat since the inaugural 1999 municipal elections. Al Rashedi retained his municipal council seat in the 2015 elections when the constituency was renumbered to constituency no. 28. His electoral success demonstrates the family's local political influence in the Al Ghuwariyah region during the 2010s. There is also a reference to a Hadi Rashedi who was executed in Iran in 2014, though the connection to the Qatari Al Rashedi family is unclear from available sources. The research also mentions a Din Muhammad Mubarez Rashedi who served in the Afghan government, but again any familial connection remains unestablished.

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Matar family origins and history. Matar Ebrahim Ali Matar, a Bahraini politician born in 1976 in Al Daih village, represents one branch of this family. His father Ebrahim is married to three women and has 17 children besides Matar, indicating a large extended family structure. The family demonstrates political diversity, with members holding varying ideological positions including leftist, Islamist, Communist, conservative, and liberal views, and many are active in opposition groups like Al Wefaq (Shia) and Wa'ad (secular). The research also mentions Ismail Matar Ibrahim Khamis Al Mukhaini Al Junaibi, an Emirati footballer whose origins trace to the Al Mukhaini family from Sur city in Oman, though this appears to be a different Al Matar lineage. data.history: Al Huwaila was an ancient town in Qatar's Al Shamal municipality that served as Qatar's main settlement from perhaps as early as the 16th century until the 18th century. Ottoman records from 1555 mention that the Qatar Peninsula was ruled by Mohammed bin Sultan bani Muslim of the Al Musallam tribe, who had his seat of power in Al Huwaila. The town's name derives from the Arabic word tahawala meaning 'to divert', referring to the area's low elevation which allowed it to divert water flow from a nearby valley. Following the founding of Zubarah in the 1760s by the Bani Utbah, the Al Musallam of Al Huwaila held nominal authority over northern settlements by taxing their residents, but were challenged by the Al Khalifa who constructed Qal'at Murair in 1768 and began refusing to pay taxes. After several skirmishes, the Al Khalifa launched a successful assault on Al Huwaila, leading to the flight of the Al Musallam leadership to Najd. In the early 19th century, the settlement was the site of the Battle of Al Huwaila, an internal conflict within the ruling Al Khalifa family of Bahrain, where three sons of Sheikh Abdullah bin Ahmad Al Khalifa took residence in Al Huwaila with support from their maternal relatives, the Al Bin Ali tribe, before being defeated around 1820. By the 1820s, the town was inhabited by about 450 people of the Al Kuwari tribe and was subject to Bahrain, but by 1887 it was found to be deserted, marking the end of its role as a major settlement.
data.description: Al Huwaila was an abandoned town in Qatar's Al Shamal municipality that served as Qatar's main settlement before the 18th century. It was the seat of power for the Al Musallam tribe until conquered by the Al Khalifa, and later became the site of an internal Al Khalifa family conflict before being permanently abandoned by 1887.
data.originStory: The town was established as the seat of power for Mohammed bin Sultan bani Muslim of the Al Musallam tribe, with the earliest known mention in Ottoman records from 1555 when he ruled the Qatar Peninsula from Al Huwaila.

data.history: The Al Marafie family is one of Kuwait's prominent historical families with origins tracing back to the late 18th century, coming from Bandar Mahshahr in Arabistan (Khuzestan). They are part of Kuwait's Ajam community - Kuwaiti citizens of Iranian descent. Marafi Behbehani was one of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century, establishing the family's early commercial presence. The family played a pioneering role in introducing modern technology to Kuwait, with M. Ma'arafie bringing the first telephone to Kuwait, establishing the first radio agency in 1935, and importing the first refrigerator in 1934. In the 1950s, approximately 40-50,000 older generations of Persian immigrants, including the Marafi family, adopted Kuwaiti citizenship to maintain their economic stronghold in the country. The family has maintained substantial economic influence and established one of Kuwait's oldest husseiniyas, Husseiniyat Marafi, founded in 1905. Modern family members continue to be active in politics and business, with Dawood Sulaiman Marafie serving as a Member of Parliament elected in 2023 and appointed as Minister of State in 2024. The family commissioned the construction of Al-Hashemi-II, the world's largest wooden dhow, which cost over $30 million and was built by Husain Marafie.
data.description: A prominent Kuwaiti family of Iranian (Ajam) origin, historically significant in commerce and modern technology introduction, with continued influence in politics and business.
data.originStory: The Al Marafie family originated from Bandar Mahshahr in Arabistan (Khuzestan province of Iran) in the late 18th century, migrating to Kuwait as part of the early Persian merchant community.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant political and business influence in Kuwait, with recent political representation through Dawood Marafie who serves in Parliament and held ministerial positions in 2024. The family has substantial economic interests and cultural contributions including maritime heritage projects.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the only verified connection to the Al Ajlan family name comes from a reference to Ajlan al-Ajlan, who serves as Head of the Saudi Arabian Commercial Chamber and called for boycotts against Turkish goods in 2020. This occurred in response to Turkish President Erdoğan's accusations against Saudi Arabia and the UAE regarding destabilization in the Caucasus and Middle East, following Al Arabiya's interview with Armenian President Armen Sarkissian during the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh conflict. Interestingly, there is also a historical connection to the name 'Ajlan' through the 7th-century Arab military commander Amr ibn al-As, who owned an estate called 'Ajlan' in Palestine, named after one of his mawālī (non-Arab, Muslim freedmen). This estate was located in the vicinity of Bayt Jibrin and remained in the possession of Amr ibn al-As's descendants as late as the 10th or 11th centuries. However, any direct genealogical connection between the historical estate name and the modern Al Ajlan family is not established in the available research.

data.history: The Al Sahaf family has documented origins in at least two distinct regions of the Middle East. In Iraq, the most prominent member is Mohammed Saeed al-Sahhaf (born 1940), who served as Iraq's Foreign Minister from 1992 to 2001 and later as Information Minister during the 2003 invasion of Iraq. Born in Hilla near Karbala to a Shi'ite Arab family, al-Sahhaf studied journalism at Baghdad University and earned a master's degree in English literature before joining the Ba'ath Party in 1963. He served as ambassador to Sweden, Burma, the United Nations, and Italy before his ministerial appointments. Separately, in Saudi Arabia, the Al Sahaf family (also spelled Al Sahafy) is documented as inhabiting the city of Ghran in Makkah Province. This branch descends from the Zabid lineage of the Harb tribe and has been the primary inhabitants of Ghran, located about 45 kilometers northeast of Jeddah. The city, formerly known as 'Green Valley,' houses approximately 14,900 people and contains historical landmarks including Al-Kadeed water near the Princess Mosque on the ancient migration road.
data.description: The Al Sahaf family appears in both Iraq and Saudi Arabia, with the Iraqi branch producing a prominent diplomat and the Saudi branch being established inhabitants of Ghran city, descending from the Harb tribe.
data.originStory: In Saudi Arabia, the Al Sahaf family descends from the Zabid lineage within the larger Harb tribe and became the primary inhabitants of Ghran city in Makkah Province.
data.modernStatus: The Iraqi branch gained international prominence through Mohammed Saeed al-Sahhaf's diplomatic career and role during the 2003 Iraq invasion. The Saudi branch maintains residence in Ghran city as established inhabitants.

data.history: The Al Ghawi family is documented in connection with the Sheikh Jarrah neighborhood of East Jerusalem, where they have been residents since at least the mid-20th century. According to Wikipedia sources on the Sheikh Jarrah controversy, the Hanoun and Ghawi families were evicted from two homes in Sheikh Jarrah in August 2009 following a Supreme Court ruling that determined the property was owned by Jews. The family had been living in housing units constructed in 1956 for Palestinian refugees who had been displaced from their original homes in Haifa, Jaffa, and other areas during the 1947-1949 Palestine war. The eviction was part of a broader legal and political dispute over property ownership in East Jerusalem, with Israeli courts recognizing Jewish claims to pre-1948 property while Palestinian families were denied similar rights to reclaim their pre-1948 properties in Israel proper. The Al Ghawi family's case became emblematic of the larger Sheikh Jarrah controversy, which has been described as a microcosm of Israeli-Palestinian land disputes since 1948.
data.description: A Palestinian family documented as residents of Sheikh Jarrah, East Jerusalem, who were evicted from their home in 2009 as part of the ongoing property disputes in the neighborhood.

data.history: The Al Sharif family encompasses multiple distinct lineages across different regions, with no single unified origin or history. The research documents several separate Al Sharif families with different backgrounds and geographic locations. The Sharif al-Ulama family are descendants of Mir Abd al-Baqi Musavi, a prominent religious scholar from Rasht in Iran's Gilan province who was granted the title 'Sharif al-Ulama' by the shah. This family traces their lineage back 15 generations to the mystic and poet Sayyid Sharafshah Dulabi (circa 1390), who was himself claimed to be a descendant of Imam Musa al-Kazim. The family established themselves as influential Islamic scholars in their local area, with descendants of Sayyid Mohammad Jan and Sayyid Moula Jan being granted the area of Ziabar by local rulers after returning from Najaf. In Palestine, the research documents the Al-Sharif family of journalist Anas Al-Sharif, whose family was displaced from Al-Majdal (now Ashkelon, Israel) in 1948 and settled in the Jabalia refugee camp in Gaza. Additionally, there are references to contemporary figures like Abdullah El Sharif, an Egyptian media personality and activist, and the Pakistani Sharif family led by former Prime Ministers Nawaz and Shehbaz Sharif, though these appear to be distinct family lineages sharing only the 'Sharif' surname.
data.description: Al Sharif represents multiple distinct family lineages across different regions, including Iranian religious scholars, Palestinian refugees, Egyptian media figures, and Pakistani political dynasties, with no unified origin or connection beyond the shared surname.

The Palestinian Al-Sharif family was displaced from Al-Majdal in 1948.
data.modernStatus: The Iranian branch maintains religious scholarly traditions, the Palestinian branch includes journalists and refugees, Egyptian branch includes media activists, and the Pakistani branch holds major political leadership including Prime Ministers.

data.history: The Bin Jassim family represents a prominent branch of Qatar's ruling Al Thani dynasty, tracing their lineage to Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani (c. 1825-1913), the founder of the modern State of Qatar. Jassim bin Mohammed emerged as a unifying leader who successfully navigated between Ottoman and British imperial pressures, ultimately establishing Qatar's independence through victories such as the Battle of Al Wajbah in 1893. The Al Thani family itself originated from the Banu Tamim tribe of Najd, settling in Qatar around the 1720s, first in Sikak, then moving to Zubarah, Al Ruwais, Fuwayrit, and finally to Doha in the 19th century under Mohammed bin Thani. The family established three main branches: Bani Ali, Bani Hamad, and Bani Khalid. Today, the Bin Jassim branch includes multiple notable political and business figures, including former Prime Minister Hamad bin Jassim bin Jaber Al Thani (known as HBJ), who served from 2007-2013 and oversaw Qatar's $230 billion sovereign wealth fund, and current Prime Minister Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani, who has played crucial roles in international diplomacy including Gaza ceasefire negotiations. The family has evolved from tribal leadership to modern constitutional monarchy, though retaining significant autocratic powers, and has accumulated vast international business holdings, particularly in London's Mayfair district.
data.description: The Bin Jassim family is a prominent branch of Qatar's ruling Al Thani dynasty, descended from Qatar's founder Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani. They hold key positions in Qatar's government and have extensive international business interests.
data.originStory: The Al Thani family originated from the Banu Tamim tribe in the Najdi town of Ushaiger, northwest of Riyadh. They settled at the Gebrin oasis in southern Najd before moving to Qatar around the 1720s, initially settling in Sikak and gradually moving northward through Zubarah, Al Ruwais, and Fuwayrit before establishing themselves in Doha in the 19th century under Mohammed bin Thani.
data.modernStatus: The Bin Jassim branch holds key positions in Qatar's government, including the current Prime Minister Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani. Former Prime Minister Hamad bin Jassim bin Jaber Al Thani remains one of the world's wealthiest individuals with extensive holdings in London real estate including the Shard, Harrods, and significant portions of Mayfair district.

data.history: The Bin Saeed family represents a distinguished branch of the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai, with their lineage tracing directly to Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum (1878-1958), who served as Dubai's longest-serving ruler from 1912 until his death in 1958. Sheikh Saeed presided over Dubai during the collapse of the pearling industry and the difficult economic transition that followed, transforming the emirate from a pearling center into an active trading hub that developed new markets and economic opportunities. His rule was marked by significant challenges including a plague outbreak in 1913, the gradual decline of the pearl trade from 1910 onwards, and internal political tensions that culminated in the Majlis movement of 1938-1939. Despite these difficulties, Sheikh Saeed laid the groundwork for Dubai's modern development, concluding agreements with Imperial Airways in 1934 and signing preliminary oil concession agreements in 1937. The family's most prominent modern representative is Sheikh Ahmed bin Saeed Al Maktoum, born December 1, 1958, just two months after his father's death. As the youngest son of Sheikh Saeed and his only child with his second wife Sheikha Fatima bint Ahmed bin Suliman, Ahmed has become one of the most influential figures in global aviation and Dubai's economic development. Since 1985, he has served as Chairman and CEO of Emirates airline, transforming it into one of the world's leading carriers, while simultaneously holding key positions including chairman of Dubai Airports, Dubai World, and Emirates NBD.
data.description: The Bin Saeed family is a prominent branch of Dubai's ruling Al Maktoum dynasty, descended from Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum who ruled Dubai from 1912-1958. The family is best known today through Sheikh Ahmed bin Saeed Al Maktoum, Chairman and CEO of Emirates airline since 1985.
data.originStory: The family originates from Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum, son of Maktoum bin Hasher who ruled Dubai from 1894 to 1906. Following the death of his cousin Butti bin Suhail Al Maktoum in 1912, Saeed smoothly acceded to power at age 34, inheriting a small but thriving trading port and pearling center that employed some 7,000 men in the seasonal trade.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant influence through Sheikh Ahmed bin Saeed Al Maktoum, who serves as Chairman and CEO of Emirates airline and the Emirates Group, second vice chairman of the Executive Council of Dubai, president of the Dubai Civil Aviation Authority, and chairman of Dubai Airports, Dubai Holdings, Dubai World, and Emirates NBD. He is also a board member of the Investment Corporation of Dubai and chancellor of The British University in Dubai.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Saeed' (سعيد) is an Arabic given name meaning 'blessed, lucky, joy' or 'happy, patient', derived from the Arabic verb sa'ada meaning 'to be happy, fortunate or lucky'.

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Nofal family, primarily focusing on Ayman Nofal, a Palestinian senior commander in Al-Qassam Brigades who was killed in October 2023. Ayman Nofal was born on May 13, 1974, and became a prominent military leader within Hamas's armed wing. By 2008, he had risen to become a field commander in central Gaza and was later arrested in Egypt along with 200 other Palestinians. After escaping from al-Marj Prison during the 2011 Egyptian revolution, he returned to Gaza and continued his military career, eventually becoming a member of the Al-Qassam Brigades' General Military Council and head of the Central Gaza Brigade. The research also mentions Nofal Nofiendra, who serves as Deputy Speaker 2 in the West Kalimantan Regional House of Representatives in Indonesia, representing the Gerindra party. However, the connection between these individuals and any broader Nofal family lineage is not established in the available research.
data.description: Limited research available focusing primarily on Palestinian military commander Ayman Nofal and an Indonesian politician Nofal Nofiendra, with no established family connections or comprehensive genealogical information.
data.modernStatus: The research indicates individual achievements rather than family-wide status, with Ayman Nofal having been a senior Hamas military commander and Nofal Nofiendra serving as a deputy speaker in Indonesian regional government.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Hooti family shows documented presence in the Gulf region through several individuals, though comprehensive family origins and migration patterns are not established in the provided sources. Rashed Al-Hooti, born in 1989, is a notable Bahraini footballer who gained international attention for receiving the fastest red card in FIFA World Cup qualification history - carded in 37 seconds (some sources say 39 seconds) during a match against Iran on October 11, 2011. This incident occurred during 2014 FIFA World Cup qualification and remains a record in international football. The family also includes Mohammed Al-Hooti, an Omani sprinter born in 1972 who competed in the 200 metres at both the 1996 and 2000 Summer Olympics, and Mohammed Huwaidi Al-Hooti, another Omani athlete born in 1986 who plays as a goalkeeper for Al-Nahda Club and has represented Oman's national team since 2007. The presence of Al Hooti family members in both Bahrain and Oman suggests possible historical connections between these Gulf states, though the specific origins, tribal affiliations, and migration patterns of the family are not documented in the available research.
data.description: A Gulf family with documented presence in Bahrain and Oman, known primarily through modern athletic achievements including a world record holder and Olympic competitors.

data.history: The Al Fazari family name derives from the Arabic الفزاري (Al-Fazari), which has historical connections to ancient Arabian tribal lineages. The most prominent contemporary member is Mohammed Al-Fazari, a political activist and media figure who was born and educated in Oman. He completed his undergraduate studies at Sultan Qaboos University in Oman before pursuing advanced degrees at the University of London's School of Oriental and African Studies (SOAS), obtaining a master's degree in Middle East politics and a doctorate in political science and international studies. Al-Fazari became a significant figure during the 2011 Omani protests, part of the broader Arab Spring movement, where he participated in demonstrations in front of the Shura Council in Muscat. His political activism led to multiple arrests and persecution by Omani authorities between 2012 and 2014, including charges of forming an organization to overthrow the government and insulting Sultan Qaboos. After founding Muwatin Media Network in June 2013, Al-Fazari faced continued harassment and was eventually forced into exile in the United Kingdom in July 2015, where he sought political asylum. From his base in the UK, he has continued his work as CEO and editor-in-chief of Muwatin Media Network and has authored several books on Omani politics and Middle Eastern affairs, including academic works published by major international publishers like Palgrave Macmillan.
data.description: The Al Fazari family is represented prominently by Mohammed Al-Fazari, an Omani political activist, author, and media executive who became a leading figure in the 2011 Omani protests and subsequently sought asylum in the UK due to persecution by Omani authorities.
data.modernStatus: The family's modern prominence comes through Mohammed Al-Fazari's role as CEO and editor-in-chief of Muwatin Media Network, his status as a political refugee in the UK, and his work as an author and political commentator on Gulf affairs.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Fazari (الفزاري) is an Arabic surname with potential historical tribal connections, though specific etymological details are not provided in the available research.

data.history: The Al Noufali family is documented through Khalifa Ayil Salim Al-Noufali, an Omani footballer born on 1 March 1984. Khalifa began his career with Oman Club before moving internationally to clubs in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, and returning to Oman. His professional football career spanned multiple Gulf countries from the early 2000s through 2016, representing Oman in international competitions including the Arabian Gulf Cup, AFC Asian Cup, and FIFA World Cup qualifications. He played for various clubs including Al-Riyadh (Saudi Arabia), Al-Sadd (Qatar), Al-Ittifaq (Saudi Arabia), Al-Kuwait (Kuwait), and several Omani clubs including Dhofar, Al-Nahda, and Bowsher Club. His international career included participation in multiple Arabian Gulf Cup tournaments from 2004-2019, notably contributing to Oman's first-ever final appearance in the 17th Arabian Gulf Cup in 2004 where they lost to Qatar in a penalty shootout.

data.history: The Al Hunaini family traces its origins to the Harb tribe, specifically belonging to the Hananyah (Al-Hunaini) branch under the Al-Marawihah section of Banu Salim. The Harb tribe is a Qahtanite Arab tribe that originated in southern Arabian Peninsula and migrated to the Hejaz region around 131 AH (approximately 749 CE) due to tribal conflicts and the search for water and land. After three centuries of settlement, the Harb tribe became dominant in the heart of Hejaz, particularly in territories surrounding the holy city of Medina. The Al Hunaini branch, as part of this larger tribal confederation, would have been established in these central Arabian territories. In recent decades, like many other tribal families, members of Al Hunaini have migrated to major metropolitan centers including Riyadh, Jeddah, Al-Qassim, and Dammam, following broader urbanization patterns in Saudi Arabia.
data.description: Al Hunaini is a family branch of the Harb tribe, specifically from the Hananyah (Al-Hunaini) lineage under the Banu Salim section. They are part of the larger Qahtanite tribal confederation with historical roots in the Hejaz region of Saudi Arabia.
data.originStory: The Al Hunaini family originates from the Harb tribe, which traces its lineage to Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan. The tribe migrated from southern Arabian Peninsula to Hejaz around 131 AH following tribal conflicts with their cousins Banu Ar-Rabi'ah bin Saad over water and land resources.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary members have largely urbanized, migrating to major Saudi metropolitan centers. The family maintains traditional tribal identity while adapting to modern Saudi society.

data.history: The Al Mahdi family represents a prominent Sudanese political and religious lineage tracing back to Muhammad Ahmad (1844-1885), who proclaimed himself the Mahdi in 1881 and led a successful revolt against Egyptian rule in Sudan. After Muhammad Ahmad's death in 1885, his posthumous son Abdul Rahman al-Mahdi (1885-1959) emerged as the leader of the Neo-Mahdist movement under British colonial rule. Despite initial restrictions, Abdul Rahman built considerable wealth through cotton production and became a significant political figure, founding the Umma Party in 1945 and advocating for Sudanese independence rather than union with Egypt. The family's political influence continued through Abdul Rahman's grandson Sadiq al-Mahdi (1935-2020), who served as Prime Minister of Sudan twice (1966-1967 and 1986-1989) and remained head of the National Umma Party and Imam of the Ansar religious sect until his death from COVID-19 in 2020. The Al Mahdi family has maintained its position as leaders of both the Umma Party and the Ansar religious movement, with Sadiq's daughter Mariam currently leading the National Umma Party.
data.description: A prominent Sudanese political and religious family descended from Muhammad Ahmad, the self-proclaimed Mahdi who led Sudan's revolt against Egyptian rule in the 1880s. The family has maintained leadership of both the Umma Party and Ansar religious sect for over a century.
data.originStory: The Al Mahdi family originates from Muhammad Ahmad (1844-1885), a Sudanese sheikh who proclaimed himself the Mahdi (messianic saviour of Islam) in 1881 and successfully led a revolt against Egyptian rule in Sudan. After capturing Khartoum in 1885 and killing General Gordon, Muhammad Ahmad died of typhus shortly after his victory, leaving behind a posthumous son Abdul Rahman who would continue the family's religious and political legacy.
data.modernStatus: The family remains influential in Sudanese politics through leadership of the National Umma Party, currently led by Mariam al-Mahdi (daughter of the late Sadiq al-Mahdi). They continue to serve as religious leaders of the Ansar sect, which pledges allegiance to Muhammad Ahmad's Mahdist movement.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Mahdi derives from the Arabic root meaning 'the Guided One' or 'divine guidance,' referring to Muhammad Ahmad's claim to be the Mahdi, the messianic figure in Islamic belief who would appear at the End of Times to rid the world of evil and injustice.

data.history: The Al Mudarris family is an Iraqi-Iranian Shia clerical family that settled in Najaf, then Karbala, from Mashhad in the early 20th century. The family claims agnatic descent from Muhammad's daughter Fatimah through her great-grandson Zayd, carrying the honorific title of Sayyid. The family branched off from the renowned cleric Muhammad-Baqir Golpayegani (1815-1897), who was born in Golpayegan and became one of the most prominent scholars of Mashhad. His son Muhammad-Jawad adopted the epithet 'al-mudarris' (the teacher) for being a prominent teacher at the seminary of Mashhad, and became Muhammad-Jawad al-Modarresi. Muhammad-Jawad left Iran for Iraq in 1936 after the Kashf-e Hijab decree and died there, buried in the Wadi al-Salam cemetery. His son Muhammad-Kadhim al-Modarresi settled in Karbala and married the daughter of the highest ranking cleric in Karbala, Mirza Mahdi al-Shirazi, solidifying his position among senior clerics. Like other clerical families, the Al Modarresi family struggled under Baathist Iraq, with Muhammad-Taqi al-Modarresi establishing the Risali Movement in 1967 under his maternal uncle's guidance. By 1971, facing government harassment, the Modarresis migrated to Kuwait, then moved to Iran after the 1979 Islamic Revolution, where they gained significant influence and established a seminary in Tehran called the Hawza of al-Qa'im.
data.description: An Iraqi-Iranian Shia clerical family that migrated from Mashhad to Iraq in the early 20th century, becoming prominent religious scholars and political activists in Karbala and later establishing influential seminaries and political organizations.
data.originStory: The family has an ethnic Arab background tracing back to imamzadeh Muhammad al-Mahrooq, the great-grandson of the fourth Shia Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn, though they settled in Mashhad, Iran before migrating to Iraq.
data.modernStatus: Muhammad-Taqi currently resides in Iraq as the leading grand Ayatollah in Karbala, while family members are spread between Qom, Mashhad, and Australia. They run the religious satellite channel Ahlulbayt TV and operate various seminaries and educational institutions.
data.nameEtymology: The family name derives from 'al-mudarris' meaning 'the teacher,' adopted by Muhammad-Jawad for being a prominent teacher at the seminary of Mashhad.

data.history: The Al Hashimi family encompasses several distinct lineages across the Middle East, with multiple branches claiming descent from the Banu Hashim tribe of the Prophet Muhammad. The most documented branch produced prominent Iraqi political figures, including Yasin al-Hashimi (1884-1937), who served twice as Prime Minister of Iraq during the mandate period. Born as Yasin Hilmi Salman in Baghdad, his father Sayyid Salman was mukhtar of the Barudiyya Quarter and claimed Hashemite descent, though historian Muhammad Y. Muslih disputes this, suggesting the family descended from the Turkish Karawiyya tribe that settled in Iraq in the 17th century. Yasin adopted the surname 'al-Hashimi' while at the Ottoman Military Academy, referencing the prestigious Banu Hashim lineage. His brother Taha al-Hashimi also served as Prime Minister of Iraq in 1941, establishing the family as influential in early Iraqi politics. In contemporary times, other individuals using the Al Hashimi name have appeared in various contexts, including Tariq al-Hashimi (born 1942), who served as Vice President of Iraq from 2006-2012, and the ISIS leader Abu Ibrahim al-Hashimi al-Qurashi (1976-2022), though their connections to the original family lineage are unclear or disputed.
data.description: Multiple Middle Eastern families claiming Hashemite descent, most notably an Iraqi political dynasty that produced several prime ministers and government officials during the 20th century.
data.originStory: According to family claims, descended from the Banu Hashim tribe of the Prophet Muhammad, though historians dispute this, suggesting possible Turkish Karawiyya tribal origins with settlement in Iraq during the 17th century.
data.modernStatus: Historically significant in Iraqi politics with diminished contemporary influence; various unrelated individuals continue to use the Al Hashimi name across the region.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Hashimi refers to descent from the Banu Hashim, the tribe of the Prophet Muhammad, though this claim is disputed for some family branches.

data.history: The Al Dossari family is a Gulf Arab family with documented presence across multiple GCC countries, particularly Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait. The research shows diverse branches of the family engaged in various sectors including media, sports, politics, and business throughout the 20th and 21st centuries. Notable figures include Jumah Mohammed Abdul Latif Al Dossari, a Bahraini citizen who was detained at Guantanamo Bay from 2002-2007, and Salman bin Yousuf Al Dossary, who serves as Saudi Arabia's Minister of Media as of 2023. The family name appears consistently across the Gulf region in various professional and public contexts, suggesting established regional presence spanning generations. Modern family members have achieved prominence in journalism, with Salman Al Dossary serving as former editor-in-chief of Asharq al-Awsat newspaper, and in sports, with footballer Khamis Al-Owairan Al-Dossari representing Saudi Arabia in FIFA World Cups. The family's geographic distribution and professional diversity indicates successful integration into Gulf society across multiple countries and sectors.
data.description: Al Dossari is a Gulf Arab family with documented presence across Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait, with notable figures in media, politics, sports, and other professional fields. The family demonstrates regional prominence through various branches engaged in journalism, government service, and business activities.

data.history: The Al Lahim family name appears in the research primarily through the documented life and career of Abdul Rahman al-Lahim, a prominent Saudi human rights lawyer born in 1971 in the deeply conservative Qassim region, specifically in the Shammasiya district. Born with a physical disability that did not deter his professional pursuits, al-Lahim represents a modern trajectory of the family from traditional conservative roots to prominent legal advocacy. His educational path took him from sharia studies at Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University in Qassim, graduating in 1997, to specialized legal studies at the Institute of Administration in Riyadh, where he obtained a diploma in systems studies focused on law in 2001. His career evolution from Arabic teacher and Islamist activist in the As-Sahwa al-Islamiyya movement in the late 1990s to becoming one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent human rights lawyers represents a significant transformation that may reflect broader family or regional changes. The family's connection to the Qassim region, particularly the Shammasiya area, suggests deep roots in this historically conservative part of central Saudi Arabia, though the research provides no information about earlier generations or the family's origins prior to the 20th century.
data.description: The Al Lahim family is documented through Abdul Rahman al-Lahim, a prominent Saudi human rights lawyer from the Qassim region who gained international recognition for defending civil rights cases. The family appears to have roots in the conservative Shammasiya district of Qassim province.
data.modernStatus: The family is prominently represented in the legal profession through Abdul Rahman al-Lahim, who has achieved international recognition as a human rights lawyer, receiving awards including the American Bar Association's 2008 International Human Rights Lawyer Award and the Human Rights Watch Award for 2008. He operates his own legal practice in Riyadh and has established a legal aid unit within his office to provide assistance to those unable to afford legal representation.

data.history: The Al Hakami family traces its origins to the Banu al-Hakam ibn Sa'd al-Ashira tribe, a branch of the Madh'hij tribal confederation, historically based in the Arabian Peninsula. The most prominent early figure was Abu Uqba al-Jarrah ibn Abdallah al-Hakami, an Arab nobleman and general who served as governor of multiple provinces including Basra, Sistan, Khurasan, Armenia and Adharbayjan during the early 8th century. Al-Jarrah was born in Jund al-Urdunn (military district of Jordan) and likely followed Abd al-Rahman ibn Habib al-Hakami to Iraq in 696 CE, establishing the family's presence in the region. The Al Hakami maintained their tribal identity and produced notable religious scholars in later centuries. In modern times, members of the Al Hakami family have been involved in Islamic scholarship and education, with some serving as teachers and religious authorities in Saudi Arabia's southwestern regions, particularly around Abu Arish in Jizan Province where the family has historical connections.
data.description: The Al Hakami family is a tribal Arabian lineage descended from the Banu al-Hakam ibn Sa'd al-Ashira, a branch of Madh'hij, known for producing early Islamic military commanders and later religious scholars. They have historical connections to southwestern Saudi Arabia, particularly the Jizan region.
data.originStory: According to historical sources, the Al Hakami family originates from the Banu al-Hakam ibn Sa'd al-Ashira tribe, a branch of the larger Madh'hij tribal confederation. The family's documented history begins in the late 7th century when members migrated from Jordan to Iraq, establishing themselves as prominent military and administrative figures in the early Islamic state.

data.history:
The Shammar (Arabic: شَمَّر) is a large Arab tribal confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated from Yemen to the northern Arabian Peninsula in the second century CE. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in 115 CE, with these mountains later becoming known as Jabal Shammar from the 14th century when the tribe adopted the Shammar name. The tribe is composed of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah, with their traditional seat of leadership in the city of Ḥaʼil in what is now Saudi Arabia.

In the 17th century, a large section of Shammar migrated to Iraq under the leadership of the Al Jarba clan, eventually becoming one of Iraq's largest tribes. The Shammar that remained in Arabia established the powerful Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1836 under the Al Rashid dynasty, which ruled from Ḥaʼil and became the primary rivals of the House of Saud. During their golden age around the 1850s, the Shammar controlled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the Syrian frontiers. The emirate was conquered by Ibn Saud in 1921, ending centuries of Shammar political dominance in the region.

Today, the Shammar maintain a strong tribal identity across multiple countries, with major populations in Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Syria, and Jordan. In Iraq, they number over 1.5 million and were historically supportive of the Hashemite monarchy, later welcoming Ba'athist rule. A Shammar leader, Ghazi al-Yawar from the Al Jarbah clan, served as Iraq's interim president in 2004. Despite modernization and urbanization, the tribe continues to play significant roles in regional politics and maintains its traditional social structures.
data.description: The Shammar is a major Arab tribal confederation descended from the Tayy tribe, originating from Yemen and settling in northern Arabia in the 2nd century CE. They are one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes, historically ruling the Emirate of Jabal Shammar and now spread across Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Syria, and Jordan.
data.originStory: The Shammar are descended from the Tayy tribe, which migrated from Yemen to the northern Arabian Peninsula in the second century CE. Led by Usma bin Luai, they invaded and settled the mountains of Ajā and Salma in 115 CE, displacing the Banu Assad and Banu Tamim. The area became known as Jabal Tayy initially, then Jabal Shammar from the 14th century when the tribe adopted the Shammar name.
data.modernStatus: The Shammar remain one of the largest Arab tribes, with significant populations across the Gulf region. In Iraq, they number over 1.5 million and have produced political leaders including former interim president Ghazi al-Yawar (2004). They maintain strong tribal identity and continue to participate in regional politics while adapting to modern urbanized lifestyles.

data.history: The Al Saeedi family name appears across multiple regions and contexts in the Middle East, with notable figures emerging from different geographical areas and religious backgrounds. In Iraq, the family produced Mushtaq Talib Al-Saeedi (1980-2024), born in Baghdad and raised in Al-Kamaliyah neighborhood, whose family originated from Diyala Governorate. He became a prominent militant commander with Harakat Hezbollah al-Nujaba and the Popular Mobilization Forces before being assassinated by a U.S. drone strike in Baghdad on January 4, 2024. In Saudi Arabia, Ali Abdullah Saleh Ali Jaber Al-Saeedi (1954-2005) represented a different branch of the family, born in Jeddah with his twin brother Salem. After his father Abdullah ibn Saleh died when Ali was 11, he pursued Islamic education and eventually became one of the most renowned Quran reciters and imams of Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, earning recognition from King Khalid and becoming known as the 'King's Imam' before his death in 2005. The research also mentions Seyyed Mohammad Saeedi as a shrine trustee at the Fatima Masumeh Shrine in Iran, indicating the family name's presence across different Islamic traditions and geographical regions, though the specific relationships between these various Al Saeedi branches remain unclear from available sources.
data.description: The Al Saeedi family encompasses multiple branches across the Middle East, including prominent religious figures in Saudi Arabia, militant commanders in Iraq, and shrine trustees in Iran.
data.modernStatus: The family has produced notable religious scholars and leaders in Saudi Arabia, militant commanders in Iraq, and religious administrators in Iran, with members holding significant positions across different Islamic institutions and political movements.

data.history: Al Mankhool is a locality in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, located in western Dubai in the area of Bur Dubai. The area is largely residential but houses several restaurants, hotels and financial service corporations including major banks such as Citibank and Emirates NBD. The locality is strategically positioned with Route D 90 (Sheikh Sabah Al Ahmad Al Jaber Al Sabah Street) running north-south through it, while Khaled bin Al Waleed Street runs perpendicular to Al Mankhool road. The section of Khaled bin Al Waleed Street in proximity to Al Mankhool is sometimes referred to as Bank Street by expatriates, reflecting the concentration of financial institutions in the area. Al Mankhool is bordered by Umm Hurair to the north, Al Rifa to the west, Al Karama to the east and Al Jafiliya to the south. Important landmarks in the area include the Al Mankhool Community Health Centre and the Bur Dubai Etisalat service centre, as well as Aster Hospitals with a facility in Mankhool.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Dahmani family name appears in historical records primarily through Salem Suhail bin Khamis Al Dahmani, a UAE policeman who served on Greater Tunb island in 1971. Al Dahmani was stationed with a six-member police force on Greater Tunb when the island was invaded by the Imperial Iranian Army on November 30, 1971, shortly before the UAE's formation. He refused to lower the flag of Ras Al Khaimah during the Iranian invasion and was killed by Iranian naval forces, making him the first recorded martyr associated with the Al Dahmani name in UAE history. His sacrifice became symbolically important for the newly formed UAE, with November 30th later designated as Commemoration Day (Martyrs' Day) in 2015 to honor his death and other UAE martyrs. The research provides no information about the family's origins, tribal connections, or broader historical presence in the Gulf region.

data.history: The Al Awad family name appears in various forms across the Middle East, with 'Awad' or 'Awwad' being an Arabic given name and surname meaning 'compensation' or 'substitute.' His father Awwad Ibrahim was a religious cleric and farmer, while his grandfather Ibrahim Ali al-Badri reportedly lived to age 94 and witnessed the US occupation of Iraq. The family was described as belonging to a Sunni Arab tribal structure that included sub-tribes such as Radhawiyyah, Husseiniyyah, Adnaniyyah, and claimed Quraysh lineage, though this latter claim lacked verification. The research also documents other Al Awad individuals including Yemeni diplomat Shaya Mohsen al-Zindani (born 1954) and Egyptian terrorist Mohamed Atta, whose full name included 'Awad' variants. These cases suggest the Al Awad name represents multiple distinct family lineages across different Arab countries rather than a single unified family structure.

data.history: The Al Dukhan family name appears in the provided research primarily through Abdul Fatah Hasan Dukhan (1936-2023), a Palestinian political figure and Hamas co-founder from Iraq Suwaydan in Mandatory Palestine. Born in 1936, he was forced to relocate to Nuseirat Camp in Gaza in 1948 during the Arab-Israeli conflict. After completing his education at UNRWA schools in Gaza and graduating from Cairo University with a geography degree, he returned to Gaza as a teacher and later became a school principal in Nuseirat Camp. His most significant contribution came during the 9 December 1987 meeting of the Palestinian Muslim Brotherhood in Gaza, where he served as the lead author of the Hamas Charter alongside other founders including Sheikh Yassin, Salah Shehade, and Yahya Sinwar. The research also mentions a geographical connection through Qatar's Jebel Dukhan, a range of limestone outcroppings that contains the country's main onshore oil deposits, with oil first discovered in the Dukhan Field in 1940, though no familial connection to the Al Dukhan family is established in the provided materials.

During the 1921-1922 period, what Al-Hulaibi referred to as the 'Divers' Uprising' took place, where divers boycotted the tribal-biased salifa court and refused to pay debts to pearl boat captains (Nakhudas). This uprising was part of the broader reform movement that transformed Bahrain's traditional economic and social structures. The Al Hulaibi family's documentation of this event suggests they were either participants in or close observers of the pearl diving industry's labor disputes. The family name appears in the context of pearl diving reforms that began in 1924, when new regulations were introduced to protect divers' interests, including requirements for separate account books for each diver and the introduction of boat-licensing taxes.

data.history: The Al Talib family traces its origins to Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib (c. 535-619 CE), who was the leader of Banu Hashim, a clan of the Qurayshi tribe of Mecca in the Hejazi region of the Arabian Peninsula. Abu Talib was the brother of Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib (father of Prophet Muhammad) and served as the uncle and guardian of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad, inheriting the position of tribal chieftain and the offices of Siqaya and Rifada after his father's death. Despite living in poverty, Abu Talib played a crucial protective role for Muhammad during the early years of Islam, defending him against persecution from other Qurayshite clans and enduring a three-year boycott that began seven years after Muhammad's first revelation. Abu Talib's descendants, known as the Alids, became central figures in Islamic history through his son Ali ibn Abi Talib, who became the fourth Rashidun Caliph and the first Shia Imam. The family lineage continued through Ali's children, including Hasan and Husayn (whose descendants are known as Hasanids and Husaynids respectively), and other sons like Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyya and Abbas ibn Ali. Under both the Umayyad (661-750) and Abbasid (750-1258) caliphates, the Alids faced severe persecution, leading many to establish regional dynasties in remote areas including the southern shores of the Caspian Sea, Yemen, and western Maghreb.
data.description: The Al Talib family descends from Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle and protector of Prophet Muhammad and leader of the Banu Hashim clan in 6th-7th century Mecca. The family's most prominent branch traces through his son Ali ibn Abi Talib, whose descendants (the Alids) became central figures in Islamic history and established various dynasties across the Muslim world.
data.originStory: Abu Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib was born in Mecca around 535 CE as the son of the Hashimite chief Abd al-Muttalib and brother of Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib. After his father's death, he inherited the leadership of Banu Hashim and took guardianship of his nephew Muhammad despite his own poverty. The family's significance grew when Abu Talib's son Ali married Muhammad's daughter Fatima, establishing the Alid lineage that would become central to Islamic succession disputes.
data.nameEtymology: Al Talib means 'of Talib' or 'belonging to Talib,' derived from Abu Talib, whose kunya (nickname) came from his firstborn son Talib. The name Ali, carried by Abu Talib's most famous son, means 'high,' 'elevated,' 'champion,' or 'king of kings' in Arabic.

data.history: Based on the limited available research, the only direct reference to the Bucheeri family appears in the context of political imprisonment in Bahrain during the 1970s. Muhammad Ghulum Bucheeri was detained alongside prominent left-wing politician Fouad Mohammed Jassim Siyadi and Saeed al-Onawaiti in November 1976. They were arrested on charges of inciting the assassination of Shiite opposition figure Sheikh Abdullah Al-Madani, which were described as spurious charges that were part of a broader campaign against the local left by the head of the National Security Agency, the British Ian Henderson. During their imprisonment, the detainees were subjected to torture, and tragically, Saeed al-Onawaiti died from the torture while Muhammad Ghulum Bucheeri and Fouad Siyadi survived their ordeal. Siyadi was eventually released in 1980, though the specific fate and release date of Muhammad Ghulum Bucheeri is not detailed in the available sources.

data.history: The Karam family is a prominent Lebanese Maronite Christian political family with deep roots in Mount Lebanon, particularly in the Ehden-Zgharta region of North Lebanon. The family's most famous member was Youssef Bey Karam (1823-1889), born to Sheikh Boutros Karam and Mariam (daughter of Sheikh Antonios Abi Khattar Al Ayntourini) in Ehden. Youssef Karam became a legendary figure in Lebanese history for his resistance against Ottoman rule, leading a rebellion from 1866-1867 and fighting in the 1860 civil conflict. His proclamations are considered early expressions of Lebanese nationalism. The family has continued its political involvement into modern times, with Salim Bey Karam serving as a current MP and former minister, following in the footsteps of his father Youssef Salim Karam who was also an MP from Zgharta. The Karam name appears across the Lebanese diaspora, with family members like Ricardo Karam becoming prominent media personalities, and the family has established foundations like the Youssef Bey Karam Foundation to preserve their patriarch's legacy.

data.history: The name Nabeel refers to multiple distinct individuals rather than a single family or tribal lineage. The most prominent figure documented is Nabeel Rajab (born 1964), a Bahraini human rights activist who serves as president of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights and is a prominent opposition figure in Bahrain's political landscape. Born to a middle-class family in Bahrain, Rajab studied Political Science and History at the University of Pune in India, graduating in 1987. His human rights activism began during the 1990s uprising in Bahrain, leading to the founding of the Bahrain Human Rights Society in 2000 and later the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights in 2002. During the 2011 Bahraini uprising, Rajab emerged as a vocal critic of human rights violations and was described as the 'unofficial leader of the 14 February movement.' His activism has resulted in multiple arrests and imprisonments, including a two-year sentence from 2012-2014 and a five-year sentence in 2018, from which he was released in 2020. Other notable individuals bearing the name include Pakistani-American Christian apologist Nabeel Qureshi (1983-2017) and Pakistani television actor Muhammad Nabeel Zafar (born 1968), though these represent separate lineages entirely.
data.description: Nabeel represents multiple distinct individuals rather than a unified family, most notably Bahraini human rights activist Nabeel Rajab and Pakistani-American author Nabeel Qureshi. The name appears across different Gulf and South Asian contexts without indicating common ancestry.
data.modernStatus: The most prominent bearer, Nabeel Rajab, remains active as president of the Bahrain Centre for Human Rights, deputy secretary general of the International Federation for Human Rights, and a member of Human Rights Watch's Middle East Division Advisory Committee, despite ongoing government persecution in Bahrain.

data.history:
Based on the research provided, there are three distinct 'Wali' families mentioned, but no single unified 'Wali family' with a coherent genealogical history. The most politically significant is the Wali Khan family of Pakistan, descended from Abdul Bahram Khan (1850-1922), founder of a major Pashtun political dynasty in the North-West Frontier Province. Abdul Bahram Khan was a landowner, farmer, and chief of the Muhammadzai tribe in Charsadda, who fought for Pashtun rights throughout his life. His sons Khan Abdul Jabbar Khan and Abdul Ghaffar Khan became prominent political leaders, with the family continuing through Abdul Wali Khan (1917-2006), a secular democratic socialist leader who opposed British rule. The family has maintained political prominence through multiple generations, with Asfandyar Wali Khan (born 1949) and his son Aimal Wali Khan (born 1986) continuing the political legacy into the 21st century.

The other 'Wali' references in the research relate to different contexts entirely: Ahmad Wali Karzai (1961-2011) was a member of Afghanistan's Karzai family from the Popalzai tribe, serving as Chairman of Kandahar Provincial Council until his assassination; and Noor Wali Mehsud (born 1978) is a militant leader from the Mehsud tribe's Mechikhel sub-clan. These are entirely separate family lineages with no connection to each other or to any GCC-based Wali family.

No evidence exists in the provided research of any Wali family having origins in or connections to the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) states. The research focuses entirely on South Asian and Central Asian political figures bearing the name 'Wali' as either a given name or title, rather than a Gulf Arab merchant or tribal family.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Jeham family is represented through Hilal Jeham Al Kuwari, who serves as President of the Aspire Zone Foundation in Qatar. The research shows his involvement in high-level sporting and diplomatic initiatives, particularly his leadership role in the January 8, 2012 pre-forum session for Doha GOALS at the Torch Hotel in Doha, Qatar. He worked alongside prominent Qatari officials including H.E. Sheikh Saoud Bin Abdulrahman Al-Thani and Hassan Al-Thawadi in developing this international sports initiative. The Aspire Zone Foundation, which he leads, was established in 2003 as an international sports project and was voted the world's leading Sports Tourism Development Project for the World Travel Awards 2011.
data.description: The Al Jeham family is represented in contemporary Qatar through leadership in major sporting institutions, particularly the Aspire Zone Foundation.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains a prominent position in Qatar's sporting sector through Hilal Jeham Al Kuwari's presidency of the Aspire Zone Foundation, an international sports project that includes Aspire academy, Aspetar and Aspire Logistics, unified by Emiri decree in 2008.
