data.history: The Al Omani family traces its roots to the Al Bu Said (Al Busaidi) dynasty, the current ruling royal family of Oman established by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi on 20 November 1744. The Busaid dynasty traces its ancestral origins to the Azd tribes through a patrilineal ancestor, al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba, with the Azd originally hailing from Yemen and migrating north after the destruction of the Marib Dam. The celebrated Umayyad general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said) is considered the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi rose to power as governor of Sohar when he successfully defended against a Persian fleet attack, holding out for nine months and forcing the Persian forces to withdraw. He was elected imam on 20 November 1744, marking the beginning of a unified Omani state and establishing a dynasty that continues to rule Oman today under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said. The dynasty ruled the extensive Omani Empire under Sultan Said bin Sultan (Said the Great) from 1804-1856, controlling territories from Zanzibar and the East African coast to parts of the Iranian coast and Balochistan. After Said's death in 1856, the empire was divided between his sons, with the Sultanate of Zanzibar going to Majid bin Said and the Sultanate of Muscat and Oman to Thuwaini bin Said, though both branches maintained the Al Busaidi lineage.
data.description: The Al Omani family belongs to the Al Bu Said (Al Busaidi) dynasty, the ruling royal family of Oman since 1744, tracing their origins to the Azd tribes of Yemen through the Umayyad general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah. The dynasty has produced numerous prominent figures in Omani government and diplomacy.
data.originStory: The Busaid dynasty traces its roots to the Azd tribes through a patrilineal ancestor, al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally hailed from Yemen and migrated north after the destruction of the Marib Dam. The progenitor of the Busaid tribe was the celebrated Umayyad general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), establishing the tribal lineage that would eventually produce the ruling dynasty of Oman.
data.tribalOrigin: Azd (through Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah lineage)
data.modernStatus: The Al Busaidi family continues as the ruling royal family of Oman under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said, who acceded to the throne in January 2020. Family members hold significant positions including Badr bin Hamad Al Busaidi as Foreign Minister since August 2020, and Crown Prince Theyazin bin Haitham who married Alia bint Mohammed Al Busaidi in April 2025, demonstrating continued intermarriage within the extended Al Busaidi lineage.
data.nameEtymology: Al Busaidi derives from 'Abu Said,' referring to the Umayyad general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah who was known as Abu Said and is considered the progenitor of the Busaid tribe.

data.history: The Al Qatifi family derives its name from Qatif, a governorate in Eastern Province, Saudi Arabia, one of the oldest settlements in the Arabian Peninsula with history dating back to 3500 BC. Qatif was historically the most important trade port in Eastern Arabia, to the point where the entire Persian Gulf was labeled 'Sea of Qatif'. The region was inhabited by indigenous Shia Muslim populations, including Arab Baharna who are the original inhabitants with very similar culture and dialects to the Baharna of Bahrain. Throughout its history, Qatif was ruled by various powers including the Qarmatians (899-1071), Uyunids (1071-1253), Usfurids (1253-1440), Jabrids (1440-1520), Portuguese (1515-1549), Ottomans (1549-1913), and finally became part of Saudi Arabia in 1913 when Ibn Saud established Saudi rule in the Eastern Province. The people of Qatif traditionally worked as merchants, farmers, and fishermen before the discovery of oil in the late 1940s transformed the economy. The region maintained its distinct Shia identity and culture, with the Qatif region being the largest concentration of Shia Islam in Saudi Arabia.
data.description: The Al Qatifi family name originates from Qatif, an ancient trading center and governorate in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, historically inhabited by indigenous Arab Baharna Shia Muslims who worked as merchants, farmers, and fishermen.
data.originStory: The family name derives from Qatif, one of the oldest settlements in the Arabian Peninsula dating back to 3500 BC. The term Qatif itself derives from what translates to 'harvest' or 'grain', signifying the area's agricultural history. The region was known by other names including Al-Khatt, and was inhabited by Arab Baharna who are the original inhabitants of the region.
data.modernStatus: Qatif is now a major oil-producing region where many residents work in the oil industry with companies like Saudi Aramco, Schlumberger, Halliburton, and Baker Hughes. The region has experienced significant development with modern infrastructure while maintaining its distinct Shia cultural identity.
data.nameEtymology: The term Qatif is derived from what translates to 'harvest' or 'grain', signifying the area's past agricultural history. The historic area was also known by other names such as Al-Khatt, immortalized in classical Arabic poetry.

data.history: The Al Yaarubi (also spelled Yaruba) were a ruling dynasty in Oman that dominated the country and prospered in the Arabian Peninsula during the late Middle Ages. The sixth Imam of Oman of the Yaruba dynasty was Saif bin Sultan II, who came to power during a period of civil war in the early 18th century and lost popularity due to his indulgent lifestyle. Facing internal challenges, Saif called for military help from Persia, and in 1737 Persian troops arrived led by Nader Shah who began to conquer the country. The Persians left in 1738 but returned from 1742 to 1744, during which time Saif was tricked into letting them take the key forts of al Jalali and al Mirani in Muscat while drunk at a banquet. After Saif's death soon after, he became the last ruler of the Yaruba dynasty. The dynasty's power finally ended in 1749 when Bal'arab bin Himyar of the Yaruba, who had been elected Imam in 1743 and retained support of some Ghafiri tribes, was defeated and killed in battle near Jebel Akhdar by Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi. This marked the definitive end of Yaruba power and the beginning of Al Bu Said rule in Oman. The Al Yaarubi legacy includes the construction of numerous forts throughout Oman, with nearly every Omani city and town having a fort that was built or had major expansions during the Al-Yarubi dynasty between 1624 and 1744, including the historical Madha Fort which is thought to have been constructed in the 17th century under the rule of Sultan bin Saif Al-Yarubi.
data.description: The Al Yaarubi were a ruling dynasty in Oman that dominated the country during the late Middle Ages and early modern period until their power ended in 1749. The dynasty ruled as Imams and were responsible for major fort construction throughout Oman between 1624 and 1744.
data.modernStatus: The Al Yaarubi dynasty ended in 1749 and no longer holds political power in Oman, having been succeeded by the Al Bu Said dynasty.
